industries.name: Fundamental Nuclear & Atomic Research
uses: Named after Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, which supplied the curium-248 target material. Produced by bombarding curium-248 with calcium-48. Sits two elements below the predicted island of stability center (Z=114, N=184). Predicted to be a heavier polonium analog but with significantly altered radioactive and chemical properties due to relativistic effects.
Longest-lived known isotope: 293Lv, t½ = 53 milliseconds. First synthesized: 2000, JINR Dubna / Lawrence Livermore collaboration — confirmed 2012.

Research probes the limits of nuclear stability, tests relativistic quantum chemistry predictions in superheavy atoms, and searches for the theoretical island of stability predicted around Z=114, N=184.
All production requires specialized particle accelerators and isotope reactor facilities — this element cannot be made by any other means.

companies.name: JINR Dubna
companies.use: Oganessian's group discovered livermorium in 2000 using curium-248 + calcium-48 at the DGFRS. Livermorium was among the first elements produced in JINR's 48Ca hot fusion program that ultimately discovered elements 113–118. The naming honored Livermore's essential role in supplying target material.

companies.name: Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
companies.use: Supplied curium-248 target material from the US nuclear stockpile and co-discovered livermorium with JINR. LLNL scientists Ken Moody, Mark Stoyer, and colleagues were integral team members in data collection and analysis. The element is named in honor of LLNL.

companies.name: Oak Ridge National Laboratory
companies.use: Provided additional curium isotope production capabilities and nuclear data support for the livermorium discovery program. ORNL's expertise in actinide isotope production and chemistry was a key resource for the US side of the Dubna-Livermore collaboration.
